topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a bright, unwelcome intrusion on my otherwise quiet afternoon. My thumb hovered over the screen, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. It was Professor Albright. A picture of him, smiling, with a caption that made my breath hitch: "Great discussion today, class!" Below it, a link to his personal Instagram profile. My heart began to thump against my ribs, a frantic rhythm I couldn't silence. I quickly minimized the window, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to force my attention back to the textbook open on my desk. Every glance toward the computer felt like a risk, a sudden, sharp awareness that he might be watching, that this digital footprint was now irrevocably linked to me. I spent the next hour staring blankly at my notes, the ink blurring into meaningless shapes.

[story 2]

It happened so casually, a simple scroll through my feed, and then there he was. Professor Albright’s profile picture, the same one from the lecture hall, was there, followed by a recent story about a weekend hike. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my hands feel clammy. I pulled my phone away as if it were suddenly hot, my jaw clenching so tightly my molars ached. I walked to the window, peering out at the familiar campus quad, but the sunlight seemed harsh, exposing everything. I felt a profound sense of exposure, a sudden vulnerability that made my skin prickle. I couldn't shake the feeling that the air in the room had suddenly grown too thin, too heavy with unspoken scrutiny.

[story 3]

I found myself checking my phone compulsively. It wasn't just one post; it was a pattern. Likes, comments, shared stories—a digital trail leading directly from the classroom to his private life. A slow, creeping dread began to coil in my chest, squeezing the air out of me. I kept glancing toward the door, half-expecting him to walk in, to see me staring at the screen. My breathing became shallow, quick little catches in my throat. I tried to focus on my essay outline, but the words swam before my eyes. The silence of my room amplified the internal clamor, a frantic need to disappear, to somehow erase the evidence of this unwanted connection.
